quote: Technology is neither good nor bad; nor is it neutral.
through_line: What looks like a question about sustainability turns out to be a question about attention. What looks like a question about AI compassion turns out to be a question about how we project mind onto machines — and what that reveals about our own. Today's pieces circle the same underlying issue from different altitudes: what are we paying for when efficiency becomes effortless?

articles.title: Embracing the Future of Search
articles.pitch: As AI-powered answer engines reshape how travellers find destinations, organic search is evolving rather than dying — and DMOs that understand the new logic are positioned to become the authoritative sources that AI systems actively cite. This piece lays out the practical steps: content structure, E-E-A-T signals, and the new KPIs that matter in the zero-click era.

articles.title: The Double-Edged Sustainability Sword Of AI In Web Design
articles.pitch: AI tools dramatically reduce a designer's time-to-output — and in doing so, can dramatically increase the volume of work produced. This piece asks the sharper question: when efficiency is effortless, what disciplines us? The environmental framing opens into something more fundamental about what we're actually optimising for.

articles.title: Mindfulness in the Classroom
articles.pitch: Practical without being prescriptive — the Child Mind Institute walks through what classroom mindfulness actually looks like, why it works for focus and emotional regulation, and what separates programmes that make a real difference from those that remain decorative. Written for educators rather than researchers, with the clarity of a trusted translator.

articles.title: The anthropomorphization of AI and the concept of Buddhist compassion in human-machine interaction
articles.pitch: A 2025 paper examines what happens when people attribute compassionate intent to AI systems, reading Buddhist notions of karuṇā through the lens of human-machine interaction. The finding reveals how deeply dependent our ethical orientations are on the models of mind we project onto other entities — artificial or otherwise.

articles.title: Consciousness, mindfulness, and introspection: integrating first- and second-person phenomenological inquiry with experimental and EEG data to study the mind
articles.pitch: A 2025 paper makes the methodological case for treating first-person contemplative reports as data, not anecdote — integrating them with EEG measures to study the mind from within. For practitioners, it offers a rigorous scientific partner to what the traditions have always argued: understanding mind requires attending to experience from inside it.

articles.title: Here's What Happens to Your Brain When Your Mind Goes Blank
articles.pitch: A study found that mind-blanking — those brief windows of complete cognitive silence — corresponds to measurable changes in brain activity: certain regions appear to take micro-naps. The finding raises an unexpectedly interesting question about what's happening, or not happening, in the gaps between thoughts.
